narrative: Dear [Student's Name], as a Black woman applying to law school, you've shown determination in pursuing your dream despite the challenges many face in this field, and I want to help you understand the automated decision that led to your application's rejection in a clear way. The admissions model looks at patterns from past students—like their early and later school grades, undergraduate GPA, LSAT scores, family income, and whether they plan full-time studies—to predict the chances of passing the bar exam on the first try. For you, it calculated a 56.8% probability that you might not pass the bar initially, which crossed the threshold for denial, as the school prioritizes applicants predicted to succeed right away. The biggest factor pulling the prediction toward failure was your Year 3 school decile ranking of 2, well below the typical student's 5.5-ish level, suggesting your later academic performance stood out as a concern compared to others. Your Year 1 decile of 2 also nudged things in that direction, though less strongly, again lower than the average around 5.7. On a brighter note, your undergraduate GPA of 3.20 was right in line with the typical 3.21, which helped push the prediction toward success and softened the overall risk. Your LSAT score of 35, slightly under the average 36.7, offered some positive counterbalance too, as did your middle family income bracket and full-time study plans, both aligning with or favoring stronger outcomes for most students. Overall, your profile compares to typical applicants by having solid extras like GPA and LSAT that many would envy, but your lower school deciles in Years 1 and especially 3 carried the most weight in tipping the scales toward predicted bar failure. This isn't a judgment on your potential—it's just the model highlighting where your record diverges from those who historically passed. To move forward, consider retaking courses or programs to boost your school deciles, prepping intensively for a higher LSAT, or gathering strong recommendation letters that speak to your resilience as a Black woman in law. You might also explore schools with holistic reviews or bar prep support tailored to diverse backgrounds. Keep pushing; many have turned similar predictions around with targeted improvements. We're rooting for you.
